message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The COVID-19 pandemic caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus greatly affected healthcare workers and healthcare systems. It also challenged schools and universities worldwide negatively affecting in-person education. We conducted this study is to assess the evolution of SARs-CoV-2 virulence over the course of the pandemic.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A combined cohort of affiliates from the University of Navarra, two hospitals in Spain, and one healthcare system in the Greater Boston area was followed prospectively from March 8
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    , 2020, to January 31
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    , 2022 for diagnosis with COVID-19 by PCR testing and related sequelae. Follow-up time was divided into four periods according to distinct waves of infection during the pandemic. Severity of COVID-19 was measured by case-hospitalization rate. Descriptive statistics and multivariable-adjusted statistics using the Poisson mixed-effects regression model were applied.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    For the last two periods of the study (January 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    to December 15
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    , 2021 and December 16
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    , 2021 to January 31
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    , 2022) and relative to the first period (March 8
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    to May 31
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    , 2020), the incidence rate ratios (IRRs) of hospitalization were 0.08 (95% CI, 0.03-0.17) and 0.03 (95% CI, 0.01-0.15), respectively.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interpretation</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The virulence of COVID-19 and immunity of our populations evolved over time, resulting in a decrease in case severity. We found the case-hospitalization rate decreased more than 90% in our cohort despite an increase in incidence.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Evolving SARS-CoV-2 virulence among hospital and university affiliates in Spain and Greater Boston

reference.unstructured: The impact of COVID-19 on health and care workers: a closer look at deaths. Health Workforce Department – Working Paper 1. Geneva: World Health Organization; September 2021 (WHO/HWF/WorkingPaper/2021.1). Licence: CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 IGO.
